initial_diagnostic_question.question: Does this ECG suggest the presence of inferior myocardial infarction?

criterion_selection.question: To accurately diagnose inferior myocardial infarction, which of the following diagnostic criteria should be evaluated?
options: Presence of ST-segment depression in leads II, III, and aVF
Presence of PR-segment depression in inferior leads
Presence of diffuse concave ST-segment elevation in limb leads
Presence of ST-segment elevation in inferior leads
criterion_selection.answer: Presence of ST-segment elevation in inferior leads

finding.question: Regarding the criterion you selected, does this ECG show ST-segment elevation in at least two of the inferior leads, including leads II, III, and aVF? Note that ST elevation is defined as an elevation of the J-point greater than 0.1mV (1mm) in these leads.

grounding.question: To verify that the inferior leads do not show ST elevation, in which of the following segments can you locate the QRS complex in these leads? The options below refer to time ranges on the ECG signal, provided in seconds.

decision.question: Based on the finding identified above, does this ECG suggest the presence of inferior myocardial infarction?
options: Further findings are required to confirm the diagnosis
decision.answer: Further findings are required to confirm the diagnosis

criterion_selection.question: In addition to the finding you just identified, which other diagnostic criterion should be evaluated to diagnose inferior myocardial infarction?
options: Presence of flattened T waves in lateral leads
Presence of pathological Q waves in inferior leads
Presence of a Delta wave (slurred upstroke) in inferior leads
Presence of pathological Q waves in leads V1, V2, V3, and V4
Presence of QS complexes in leads V1 and V2
criterion_selection.answer: Presence of pathological Q waves in inferior leads

finding.question: Regarding the criterion you selected, does this ECG show pathological Q waves in at least two of the inferior leads, including leads II, III, and aVF? Note that a pathological Q wave in these leads is defined as either (1) a QS complex, (2) a Q wave with a duration greater than 30 milliseconds and an amplitude greater than one-fifth of the following R amplitude, or (3) a deep S wave with only a small r wave preceding it.

grounding.question: Which of the following leads show the pathological Q waves? Select all possible leads from the options below.
Within the selected leads, in which of the following segments can you observe the QRS complex with pathological Q waves? The options below refer to time ranges on the ECG signal, provided in seconds.

decision.question: Based on all the findings identified so far, does this ECG suggest the presence of inferior myocardial infarction?
options: Further findings are required to confirm the diagnosis
